flashcards.front: What is the general form of recurrence relations solvable by Master Theorem?
flashcards.back: T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) where a ≥ 1, b > 1, and f(n) is asymptotically positive

flashcards.front: What does Case 2 of Master Theorem handle?
flashcards.back: When f(n) = Θ(n^log_b a (log n)^k) where k ≥ 0. Solution: T(n) = Θ(n^log_b a (log n)^(k+1))

flashcards.front: What is the critical exponent in Master Theorem?
flashcards.back: log_b a - the exponent in the n^(log_b a) term that determines case separation

flashcards.front: When does Master Theorem Case 1 apply?
flashcards.back: When f(n) is polynomially smaller than n^log_b a (f(n) = O(n^{log_b a - ε}))

flashcards.front: What is the regularity condition in Case 3?
flashcards.back: a*f(n/b) ≤ c*f(n) for some c < 1 and sufficiently large n

flashcards.front: Which algorithms typically yield T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)?
flashcards.back: Merge Sort and other O(n log n) divide-and-conquer algorithms

flashcards.front: What is the solution to T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n log n?
flashcards.back: Case 3: Θ(n log n) as f(n) dominates n^{log_4 3}

flashcards.front: Can Master Theorem handle T(n) = T(n-1) + 1?
flashcards.back: No - Master Theorem only applies to divide-and-conquer recurrences with n/b form

flashcards.front: What is the time complexity of Strassen's algorithm via Master Theorem?
What does 'a' represent in the recurrence relation?
flashcards.back: Number of subproblems generated in each recursion step

flashcards.front: How to handle T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n² log n?
flashcards.back: Case 2 with k=1: Θ(n² log² n) as f(n) = n² log n and log_b a = 2

flashcards.front: What is the limitation of Master Theorem?
flashcards.back: Cannot handle non-polynomial differences between f(n) and n^log_b a, or when a is not constant
